phase1.title: The Deep Fringe
phase1.narrative: You grew up where the Empire's reach frays to nothing — twin suns bleaching everything pale, the nearest starport a week's ride out. Self-reliance isn't a virtue here; it's the only thing keeping you breathing. The galaxy owes you nothing, and that truth made you dangerous.

_meta.favoredDesc: Years of rationing water, patching shelters against sandstorms, and reading the land for hidden dangers forged an instinct for staying alive where nothing wants you to.
_meta.knack: You read terrain for water, shelter, and danger the way city people read street signs. You know whether a settlement is thriving or dying from its water infrastructure alone. You recognize the signs of a sandstorm, a predator's territory, or a dried-up well before anyone else looks twice. In places where civilization thins out, you are never lost — you are home.

phase1.title: The Shadowed Levels
phase1.narrative: You were raised in the underlevels — where the lights of the upper city are just a rumor and debts are paid in blood or service. You learned to read people before you could read a holomap, and you know that every deal has a hidden clause. Trust is a currency you spend carefully.

_meta.favoredDesc: Moving unseen through the stacked warrens of the underworld taught you how to vanish mid-stride — slipping through crowds, ducking sensor sweeps, and leaving no trace.
_meta.knack: You read a room's angles before you finish walking through the door — who runs this place, who owes whom, where the exits are, and whether the deal being offered has a hidden clause. You know how underworld economies work: which vendors are fronts, which debts are real, and which smiles are transactions. In any settlement with a criminal layer, you see the machinery underneath.

phase1.title: The Salvage Yards
phase1.narrative: Every ship that flies was once someone's wreck. You grew up knee-deep in that wreckage — pulling circuits from crashed freighters, selling scrap to keep the lights on. Machines tell you their secrets if you know how to listen. You've built survival from the galaxy's trash.

_meta.favoredDesc: You learned to coax dying machines back to life with salvaged parts and sheer stubbornness. Every hull fragment whispers its history if you know where to press your ear.
_meta.knack: You see the history in machines. A ship's hull tells you where it has been, what hit it, and whether it was repaired by someone who knew what they were doing. You recognize salvageable tech in wreckage that others dismiss as scrap. When someone says something is broken beyond repair, you know whether that is true or whether they just do not know where to look.

phase1.title: The Coreward Spires
phase1.narrative: You had everything the Empire promised — spires that touched the clouds, an education that opened doors, and a family name worth something. Then you saw what that system cost the rest of the galaxy. Some fled privilege in guilt; others were pushed out when their usefulness expired.

_meta.favoredDesc: Growing up among senators and industrialists taught you how words shape power. You know how to frame an argument so it feels like the listener's own idea.
_meta.knack: You understand how power structures work — corporate hierarchies, political favors, the language of bureaucracy. You recognize when someone is performing authority versus actually holding it. You know the customs, protocols, and social codes of the educated elite, and you can tell the difference between old money and new money by how someone enters a room.

phase1.title: The Agrarian Plain
phase1.narrative: You come from working land — rows of crops, communal tables, and seasons that ruled your calendar. Your community survived by cooperation and quiet resilience. The Empire took the harvest and left the labor. That memory of something worth protecting is why you fight.

_meta.favoredDesc: Seasons of drought, Imperial levy collectors, and backbreaking harvests taught you to endure what would break others. When the soil fights you, you plant deeper.
_meta.knack: You read weather, soil, and seasons instinctively. You know whether a community is self-sustaining or dependent, whether a harvest will last the winter, and you recognize the signs of Imperial taxation squeezing a settlement dry — the thinning herds, the hollow granaries, the look on a farmer's face when the levy collector's ship lands. You know what cooperation holds a community together, and what happens when it breaks.

phase1.title: The War Front
phase1.narrative: Peace is something other people talk about. You grew up in the sound of it — distant artillery, rationed water, the faces of soldiers cycling through. War was the weather of your childhood. You learned to read threat before you learned to read a face. You are very, very hard to surprise.

_meta.favoredDesc: Artillery and ambushes rewired your nervous system. You don't think about dodging — your body moves before the blast hits, honed by years of living where standing still means dying.
_meta.knack: You read environments for threat before you read them for anything else. You know the difference between a patrol and a sweep, between a checkpoint and a kill zone. You recognize military hardware, unit markings, and the body language of soldiers who are about to use force versus soldiers who are just bored. In any place where violence has happened recently, you see the evidence others walk past.

phase1.title: The Ancient Ruin
phase1.narrative: Your home was built on something older — crumbled temples, inscriptions no living scholar could read, and a persistent feeling that the air itself was watching. You grew up chasing questions no one could answer. Whatever the old civilization left behind, a fragment of it lodged in you.

_meta.favoredDesc: Tracing glyphs by lamplight and cross-referencing crumbling datapads trained you to spot what others overlook. Patterns emerge for you where everyone else sees noise.
_meta.knack: You notice things that do not belong to the present — inscriptions, architectural remnants, artifacts that predate the Republic. You recognize when a site has been disturbed, when someone has been excavating, and when the age of a place does not match the official story. In any location with ancient roots, the past speaks to you in a language most people have forgotten exists.

phase1.title: The Trading Post
phase1.narrative: Where routes cross, everything flows — goods, gossip, fugitives, and opportunity. You grew up at the intersection, learning a dozen languages before adulthood and the art of the deal before you could pilot. You know that information is the most valuable cargo in the galaxy.

_meta.favoredDesc: A lifetime of reading merchants, refugees, and con artists at the crossroads gave you an ear for half-truths. You sense the angle before the pitch is finished.
_meta.knack: You speak the language of trade — literally and figuratively. You recognize the origins of goods by their markings, you know when a price is fair across multiple markets, and you read the flow of a port to know what is moving, what is scarce, and who is desperate to sell. In any settlement where commerce flows, you understand the economy before anyone explains it to you.

phase1.title: The Detention Block
phase1.narrative: You were born into a cage — not always made of durasteel. Labor camp, indentured service, political imprisonment, or simply the wrong bloodline at the wrong time. The Empire taught you exactly what it was through direct experience. Freedom, when it came, felt like a weapon handed to you.

_meta.favoredDesc: Starvation rations, forced marches, and interrogation droids taught your body to absorb punishment that would drop anyone else. Pain is just a signal you learned to override.
_meta.knack: You know how confinement works — guard rotations, the rhythm of institutional control, which rules are enforced and which are theater. You recognize the architecture of containment: where the monitoring points are, where the blind spots are, and how facilities control movement. When you walk into any place designed to hold people, you already understand its logic.

phase1.title: The Shipboard Born
phase1.narrative: You were born between jumps, raised in the hold of a freighter, and taught to read a navcomputer before you could read Basic. Ships aren't transportation to you — they're the only home you've ever had. The stars don't frighten you. The ground does.

_meta.tone: wanderlust, belonging, the void as home
_meta.favoredDesc: Born between hyperspace jumps, you feel a ship's rhythm the way grounders feel gravity. Thrust vectors, drift corrections, and emergency burns are second nature — the cockpit is the only room that ever felt like home.
_meta.knack: Ships talk to you. You hear the difference between a healthy engine and one running hot. You know a freighter's layout by its class, you can navigate a vessel in the dark by the vibration of the deck plates, and you recognize the sounds that mean something is about to go very wrong. When you board any ship, you already know its condition, its age, and whether it has been cared for or ridden hard.

phase1.title: The Labor Camp
phase1.narrative: You were a number on a corporate extraction ledger. Whether conscripted, sold into indentured service, or simply born on the wrong side of a mining contract, you spent your formative years breaking your back for quotas that were never going to be met. Your body knows pain. Your mind knows how to keep going anyway.

_meta.favoredDesc: Hauling ore carts, climbing unstable shafts, and breaking rock with crude tools sculpted a body built for raw physical output. When the ground shakes, you keep swinging.
_meta.knack: You know the economics of exploitation — work quotas, ration schedules, the calculations that treat people as output. You recognize when a community is being squeezed, when workers are being underpaid, and you see the difference between someone who has been broken by labor and someone who is about to break free. In any place where the powerful profit from the labor of others, you see the system for what it is.

phase1.title: The Enclave
phase1.narrative: You were raised inside something closed — a religious order, a hidden commune, a survivalist sect, or the remnant of an ancient tradition that survived by going silent. Inside, there was belonging and purpose. Outside was danger. When you finally crossed the threshold, the galaxy felt enormous and hostile in equal measure.

_meta.favoredDesc: Years of communal ritual and close-quarter diplomacy gave you an effortless warmth that disarms strangers. People want to trust you — and you learned exactly how to invite that.
_meta.knack: You understand closed systems — the rituals, hierarchies, and unspoken rules that hold insular groups together. You recognize when someone has left a community they loved, when a tradition is being maintained out of faith versus fear, and you know the signs of a group that is tightening its borders against outsiders. In any community held together by belief or belonging, you see the bonds and the fractures.

phase2.title: The Disbanded Regular
phase2.narrative: You fought for the Galactic Republic or a localized planetary defense force. You bled in the mud alongside Clone Troopers to hold the line against the droid army. But when the war ended, the Republic became the Empire. Your unit was either forcibly decommissioned, replaced by conscripted stormtroopers, or folded into a fascist machine you refused to serve.

_meta.favoredDesc: Years of reading battlefields — counting exits, gauging fire arcs, and timing advances — gave you an instinct for turning chaos into a plan before anyone else has stopped flinching.
_meta.variability: Disciplined squad leader (Tactics/Ranged) or heavy-infantry trench fighter (Athletics/Ranged)?
_meta.knack: You know how military organizations function — chain of command, logistics, the gap between orders and execution. You recognize military discipline in people who have served by how they stand, how they scan a room, and how they respond to sudden noise. You can assess a fighting force's readiness, morale, and command quality at a glance. Veterans see you the same way — and they know you have been in the mud.

phase2.title: The Separatist Holdout
phase2.narrative: You fought for the Confederacy of Independent Systems. You believed in independence from a corrupt Core, but you lost the war. Now, the new Empire labels you a terrorist. You spent the last four years hiding your past, keeping old Separatist tech running with scavenged parts, and maintaining a deep, burning hatred for the stormtroopers occupying your world.

_meta.favoredDesc: Defending crumbling positions against clone battalions taught you to make every shot count. You learned to shoot straight under fire when bolts were too precious to waste.
_meta.variability: Guerrilla mechanic (Tech/Ranged) or hardened survivor of the sieges (Endure/Ranged)?
_meta.knack: You recognize Separatist-era technology, insignia, and ideology even when it has been buried or disguised. You know which worlds sympathized with the Cause, which communities still harbor holdouts, and you can tell when someone's hatred of the Republic — now Empire — comes from conviction rather than convenience. In settlements with old CIS sympathies, you see the signs that have been painted over but not erased.

phase2.title: The Imperial Defector
phase2.narrative: You bought the Emperor's lies about peace and order. You enlisted in the new Imperial Academies and were shipped out to the Western Regions. But when you saw the sheer brutality of the Pacification Campaigns — the burning of settlements and the subjugation of innocents — your conscience broke. You went AWOL, taking their training and their secrets with you.

_meta.favoredDesc: You wore the Empire's face long enough to learn how authority sounds. Even after defecting, you can slip back into that cold, confident cadence — and most people never question the uniform in the voice.
_meta.variability: Rogue TIE pilot (Piloting/Tactics) or quartermaster who stole Imperial codes (Tactics/Skulduggery)?
_meta.knack: You know how the Empire operates from the inside — rank structure, comm protocols, patrol patterns, the specific laziness of Outer Rim garrison duty. You recognize Imperial equipment variants by era and theatre, you know the difference between a competent officer and a political appointee, and you understand the bureaucratic seams where procedure breaks down. When the Empire is present, you see what civilians miss.

phase2.title: The Blockade Runner
phase2.narrative: The Clone Wars were a tragedy for some, but a business opportunity for you. You spent the war running food, weapons, and medical supplies past massive Republic cruisers and Separatist blockades. You learned how to fly a freighter like a starfighter, how to lie to military customs officers, and how to dump your cargo at the first sign of a tractor beam.

_meta.tone: speed, risk, freedom at any cost
_meta.favoredDesc: Running contraband past capital ships taught your hands to fly on instinct. You thread needle-gaps in debris fields and outrun tractor beams before your conscious mind catches up.
_meta.variability: Ace smuggler pilot (Piloting/Insight) or smooth-talking contraband broker (Deception/Insight)?
_meta.knack: You read spacelanes the way navigators read charts — but for what is hidden. You know which routes are patrolled, which ports ask questions, and which customs officers look the other way. You recognize a ship that is running cargo it should not be by how it flies and where it docks. In any spaceport, you know the difference between legitimate freight and a smuggling operation before the ramp drops.

phase2.title: The Pacification Survivor
phase2.narrative: You didn't care about the Republic or the Separatists until the new Empire arrived in the Western Regions to "pacify" your home. You watched your settlement burn and your leaders get executed. You fled into the wilderness and learned to fight a desperate, asymmetrical guerrilla war against AT-DP walkers and Imperial garrisons using only traps, shadows, and scavenged blasters.

_meta.favoredDesc: When the Empire burned your home, the wilderness became your classroom. You learned to find water in ash, set traps from wreckage, and outlast garrison patrols by knowing the land better than they ever will.
_meta.variability: Wilderness ambush specialist (Stealth/Survival) or nimble hit-and-run saboteur (Stealth/Evasion)?
_meta.knack: You know what Imperial pacification looks like before it arrives — the staging patterns, the propaganda broadcasts, the sudden interest in census data. You recognize the signs of a community being prepared for subjugation, and you know which direction people flee when it starts. In any settlement under Imperial pressure, you see the fear that the residents are trying to hide, because you have worn that same face.

phase2.title: The Field Medic
phase2.narrative: The Clone Wars produced billions of casualties. You found your calling in the blood and the mud, patching up soldiers, mercenaries, and collateral civilians. You learned how to dig plasma-scorched shrapnel out of flesh with cheap tools, how to keep your hands steady while artillery shakes the ground, and how to make horrific triage choices.

_meta.tone: duty, triage, moral cost of war
_meta.favoredDesc: Digging shrapnel out of screaming soldiers while artillery shook the ceiling taught you to think in seconds. You diagnose by touch, prioritize by instinct, and never waste a stim-pack.
_meta.variability: Hardened combat surgeon (Medicine/Resolve) or perceptive, empathetic healer (Medicine/Insight)?
_meta.knack: You assess injuries by sight — severity, cause, time since infliction. You know what weapons leave what wounds, whether someone has been treated by a professional or a desperate amateur, and you can tell when someone is hiding pain behind a steady voice. You know the smell of a med-bay that is running low on supplies, and you recognize the signs of field-surgery scars on anyone who carries them.

phase2.title: The Syndicate Enforcer
phase2.narrative: The galactic war was just a distraction. While the Jedi and the Droid Armies fought, the underworld syndicates expanded their empires. You spent the last few years working for the Hutts, the Pykes, or the Crimson Dawn. You learned how to collect debts, how to take a hit from a vibro-blade, and how to project enough quiet menace to make people pay up without a fight.

_meta.favoredDesc: Collecting debts for the Hutts taught you that violence is most effective when it stays implied. A look, a silence, and the weight of what you represent make most targets cooperate before you raise a fist.
_meta.variability: Immovable heavy enforcer (Brawl/Endure) or terrifying interrogator/debt collector (Intimidate/Brawl)?
_meta.knack: You know how criminal organizations project power — the enforcers, the accountants, the figurehead versus the real boss. You recognize when someone owes a debt by how they carry themselves in a room, you know the difference between a shakedown and a genuine warning, and you understand what it means when a name is spoken quietly instead of loudly. In any place where the syndicates have reach, you see the hierarchy.

phase2.title: The Post-War Tracker
phase2.narrative: The end of the war created millions of refugees, deserters, and war criminals. The Empire and the Syndicates both put out massive bounties to clean up the mess. You took the hunter's creed, learning how to track a quarry across a dozen chaotic, war-torn star systems, and how to put a blaster bolt exactly where it needs to go to secure a live capture.

_meta.favoredDesc: Tracking war criminals across a dozen shattered systems taught you to read a cold trail — a discarded fuel receipt, a nervous docking clerk, the faintest pattern in hyperspace logs.
_meta.variability: Urban detective hunting leads (Investigation/Ranged) or relentless Outer Rim tracker (Survival/Ranged)?
_meta.knack: You read people for flight risk — the ones who check exits, the ones who pay in cash, the ones whose stories do not match their clothes. You know how people run: which routes they take, which aliases they favor, and what mistakes they make when they think they have gotten away. In any crowd, you see the person who does not want to be found, because you know every pattern of evasion.

phase2.title: The Purge Survivor
phase2.narrative: Order 66 didn't just target the Jedi; the Empire immediately outlawed dozens of religions, academic orders, and political factions. You were part of a group that was declared treasonous the day the Empire was born. For four years, you have survived by hiding your true nature, trusting your instincts over your eyes, and learning how to disappear into a crowd.

_meta.favoredDesc: Three years of hiding your true nature from Inquisitors and informants made invisibility a reflex. You move through occupied space like smoke — present, but impossible to grasp.
_meta.variability: Force-sensitive hiding their signature (Sense/Stealth) or purged political scholar under a fake identity (Deception/Stealth)?
_meta.knack: You know what persecution looks like from the inside — informant networks, loyalty tests, the slow tightening of surveillance around a community. You recognize when someone is reporting to an authority they are afraid of, when a question is really an interrogation, and when a settlement has been seeded with watchers. In any place where the Empire is hunting for something specific, you see the net before it closes.

phase2.title: The Wreck
phase2.narrative: Something catastrophic happened — a ship destroyed, a convoy ambushed, a convoy gone silent in the void. You were one of the only survivors, dragged out of the debris by luck or will alone. The wreck changed the trajectory of everything. What you lost there is still with you.

_meta.favoredDesc: Surviving the catastrophe branded endurance into your bones. Decompression, impact trauma, and days of crawling through wreckage taught your body to keep functioning long past the point of collapse.
_meta.variability: Engineer who kept the ship alive long enough (Tech/Endure) or gunner who survived the hull breach (Survival/Endure)?
_meta.knack: You know what catastrophic failure looks like — structural, mechanical, human. You recognize when a ship, a building, or a plan is about to come apart by the stress fractures that others miss. You see the hairline crack in the bulkhead, the overloaded power coupling, the team dynamic that is one bad decision away from collapse. You have lived through what happens when those fractures give way, and that knowledge never turns off.

phase2.title: The Ascent
phase2.narrative: You climbed. Whether through the Imperial ranks, a corporate hierarchy, or a criminal syndicate's chain of command, you earned real power — and then burned it all down on your way out. You know how institutions work from the inside. You know exactly where the cracks are.

_meta.favoredDesc: You climbed hierarchies by making people believe in you — or fear you. That voice of authority never left, even after you walked away from power. People still follow when you speak.
_meta.variability: Former Imperial officer with codes and contacts (Tactics/Deception) or syndicate lieutenant who went independent (Persuasion/Tactics)?
_meta.knack: You understand institutional power — how hierarchies reward loyalty, punish independence, and protect themselves from exposure. You recognize bureaucratic maneuvering, you know when someone is consolidating power versus performing it, and you can read the politics of any organization by watching who speaks, who stays silent, and who leaves the room when decisions are made.

phase2.title: The Betrayal
phase2.narrative: Someone you trusted set you up. A partner, a commanding officer, a patron — someone who smiled while selling you out. You survived what they arranged for you, and now you carry two things everywhere: the lesson, and the debt. Trust is not something you give freely anymore.

_meta.favoredDesc: Being sold out carved a permanent edge into your perception. You read micro-expressions, catch the pause before a lie, and notice the hand drifting toward a hidden weapon — because you learned what it costs not to.
_meta.variability: Intelligence operative burned by their handler (Insight/Deception) or soldier set up by their lieutenant (Skulduggery/Stealth)?
_meta.knack: You read trust like a threat assessment. You notice when someone's generosity has conditions attached, when loyalty is being performed rather than felt, and when a deal is designed to leave you holding the consequences. You know what betrayal looks like before it lands — the rehearsed sincerity, the offer that is too clean, the friend who suddenly needs to know your schedule. You learned what it costs not to see these things, and you will not pay that price again.

phase3.title: The Hutt-Marked
phase3.narrative: You owe credits to a Hutt cartel operating out of the Western Reaches. Not the Banking Clan kind of debt — the kind tracked by Weequay enforcers and Kyuzo hunters wearing wide-brimmed war helms. You carry a coded burn-mark on your wrist that any bounty hunter or cartel operative recognizes on sight. In cantinas from Jakku to Ord Mantell, that mark means you are collectable. You have learned to read a room the moment you enter — who is watching, who is armed, and who has already sent a message.

_meta.knack: You recognize syndicate affiliations on sight — tattoos, burn-marks, hand signals, the way someone positions themselves relative to the door. You know how Hutt operations are structured: who collects, who enforces, who reports up. In any settlement where the cartels operate, you know the local price of silence — who can be bribed, how much it costs, and whether it will hold.

phase3.title: The Witness
phase3.narrative: You were in the wrong place during the Pacification Campaigns. You saw an Imperial garrison do something to a frontier settlement that was not in any official report — mass relocations, unmarked cargo shipments, civilians loaded onto transports that never arrived at any registered facility. Out here in the Western Reaches, the Empire does not operate like it does in the Core. There are no journalists, no Senate hearings, no witnesses. Except you. And someone in an Imperial uniform knows your face.

_meta.knack: You have seen what the Empire does when it thinks nobody is watching. You recognize the patterns — falsified manifests, patrol routes that deliberately avoid specific buildings, officers whose rank does not match their posting, equipment that has no business at a frontier garrison. In any Imperial-controlled area, when the official story does not add up, you notice. You always notice.

phase3.title: The Traumatized
phase3.narrative: The Clone Wars or the Pacification Campaigns did not just happen near you — they happened to you. You watched settlements burn in the Western Reaches while stormtroopers stood in formation. You lost everyone, and the sounds have not stopped. Certain things — the whine of a repulsor engine, the smell of ozone after a blaster discharge, the hiss of a blast door — pull you back to the worst moment of your life without warning.

_meta.knack: You recognize the precursors to violence before they register for anyone else — the change in air pressure before an explosion, the specific silence that falls over a room before someone draws a weapon, the hum of a charging power cell. You know what a place looks like after it has been destroyed, and you see the ghost of that destruction in places that are still standing. In any situation where sudden violence is imminent, your body knows before your mind catches up.

phase3.title: The Shadow-Stalked
phase3.narrative: You are sensitive to the Force, but you have not had training since the Purge. Instead of guidance, you feel an unexplainable, persistent spiritual pressure — an Echo. It manifests as haunting premonitions of danger or the overwhelming sensation that something ancient and patient is tracking you through the currents of the universe. In the Western Reaches, far from the Core, the Force feels different — wilder, older, tangled with things the Jedi never catalogued.

_meta.knack: You feel the weight of places and people in ways you cannot fully explain. A room where something terrible happened feels wrong to you even if it looks clean. A person who intends harm carries a coldness you sense before they speak. You are drawn toward things that matter — doors, artifacts, decisions — without understanding why. The Force does not speak to you in words; it speaks in pressure, temperature, and the pull of something ancient and patient.

phase3.title: The Defector
phase3.narrative: You were part of the machine. You wore the uniform, carried the codes, and followed orders when the Empire arrived in the Western Reaches to pacify the frontier. You know how Imperial garrisons operate — shift rotations, comm protocols, supply chain logistics, the chain of command from the local garrison commander up to the sector Moff. When you walked away, you took all of that knowledge with you. If they ever find you, the charge is treason. The sentence is summary execution.

_meta.tone: treason, hunted by empire, fractured identity
_meta.knack: You know how the Empire works because you were the Empire. You understand checkpoint procedure, supply chain logistics, shift rotations, and the specific laziness of garrison duty in the Outer Rim. You know which codes are checked and which are waved through, which uniforms draw salutes and which draw suspicion, and how to speak like someone who belongs inside a facility. The difference between you and a civilian is that you do not have to guess — you remember.

phase3.title: The Debtor
phase3.narrative: You owe a debt, but not to a crime lord. This is a debt of honor — a blood oath, a life saved, or a massive ledger entry with the Banking Clan western branch office. Legitimate debt out here is almost worse than criminal debt: there is no negotiating with a protocol droid that has your biometrics on file and a legal mandate to garnish everything you earn. Until it is paid, you have no credit line, no property rights, and no home. Every settlement with a Banking Clan terminal knows your face.

_meta.knack: You understand how credits flow through the frontier — who extends credit, who launders it, who owes whom, and which settlements operate on barter because the Banking Clan has not reached them yet. You recognize a front operation by its inventory, you know when a merchant is underwater by the desperation in their pricing, and you can assess the economic pressure points of any community you walk into. Debt taught you the architecture of money.

phase3.title: The Exile
phase3.narrative: You are forbidden from returning to a specific place — a planet, a station, a community, or an organization — on pain of death. In the Western Reaches, exile is common: religious sects on jungle moons, mercenary companies that operate out of orbital stations, political families that control entire settlement networks. Whatever you were part of, they cast you out. Your name was struck. Your identity was burned. Out here on the frontier, you are nobody — and that is the only thing keeping you alive.

_meta.knack: You recognize the signs of exile because you carry them. The way someone avoids eye contact with a specific group. The settlements that will not serve certain people. The tattoos that have been burned off, the insignia that has been filed down, the accent that has been deliberately flattened. You know who is running from something, because you see your own reflection in them — and they see theirs in you.

phase3.title: The Addiction
phase3.narrative: You are dependent on a specific substance to function — a stim, a spice derivative, or a field-med cocktail left over from wartime. In the Western Reaches, supply is inconsistent: some settlements have backroom pharmacists who can synthesize what you need, others have nothing. Going without is not an option you have tested more than once. The shaking starts within a day. The clarity goes next. You have built your entire routine — where you go, who you trust, what jobs you take — around making sure you never run dry again.

_meta.knack: You know the substance economy of the frontier. You identify a dealer, a pharmacist, or a backroom supplier in any settlement — not because you are connected, but because you have had to. You recognize withdrawal in someone's hands before it reaches their face. You know the look of a person who is three days from running out, and you know what that scarcity will make them willing to trade. In any place where controlled substances move, you see the supply chain.

phase3.title: The False Identity
phase3.narrative: The person you are now is a fabrication. Your original identity was erased during the Clone Wars, the Jedi Purge, or the chaos of the Imperial transition. You possess forged documentation, a false birth record, and a backstory you have rehearsed until it feels true. In the Western Reaches, where record-keeping is spotty and Imperial databases do not reach every settlement, the lie holds. But every time you pass through a spaceport with a full Imperial customs scanner, you feel the floor shift beneath you.

_meta.tone: deception, buried past, fear of exposure
_meta.knack: You understand how identity works on the frontier — which documents matter, which settlements do not check, which Imperial systems have gaps that a forged ident-card can slip through. You recognize when someone else is living under a fabricated name, because you see the same tells you have trained out of yourself: the half-second pause before answering to the wrong name, the reflexive check when an old alias is spoken nearby. In any place where identities are verified, you know how deep the scrutiny goes.

phase3.title: The Notorious
phase3.narrative: You have a reputation that travels faster than you do. In the cantinas of Jakku, the landing pads of Ord Mantell, the swoop-gang rallies along the frontier — your name, or the name you earned, carries weight. Whether it is deserved or exaggerated does not matter. People have already decided who you are before you walk through the door. The right people buy you drinks. The wrong people reach for their blasters. You are still figuring out whether to live up to the legend or bury it before it buries you.

_meta.tone: infamy, identity, the cost of being known
_meta.knack: You know what recognition looks like — the double-take at the bar, the whispered conversation that stops when you approach, the bartender who pours your drink before you order it. In any community where word travels — cantinas, criminal networks, mercenary circles, frontier settlements — you can read the room to know whether your name has arrived before you. You see who wants something from you, who is afraid of you, and who has already sent a message to someone else about your presence.

phase3.title: The Blood Price
phase3.narrative: Someone paid for your freedom, your survival, or your escape from the Western Reaches — and the cost was something you cannot repay. It might have been their life, given during the Pacification Campaigns when your settlement was burned. It might have been their safety, traded so you could board a transport off a dying colony. You are here because they are not. The frontier is full of debts like this — everyone lost someone when the Empire arrived. Yours just has not stopped bleeding.

_meta.knack: You see the cost of sacrifice in other people before they name it. The person carrying someone else's grief, the one making decisions out of guilt rather than logic, the leader who is spending lives they cannot afford to lose — you recognize all of them, because you have been on both sides of that equation. You know what a situation looks like when someone is about to make a trade they cannot take back, and you know the silence that follows when they do.

phase3.title: The Hunted
phase3.narrative: Something is tracking you. Not the Empire in the broad bureaucratic sense — something specific, patient, and personal. A bounty hunter with a decades-old contract. An Inquisitor with a fixation. A crime lord who took your escape as an insult. Out in the Western Reaches, distance means nothing to the kind of hunter who is after you. You have learned the rhythm of pursuit: the quiet weeks that feel safe, the moment you spot a ship that should not be at a frontier port, the sick certainty that they have found you again.

_meta.tone: paranoia, animal awareness, the weight of being prey
_meta.knack: You have been hunted long enough that awareness is no longer a skill — it is a condition. You notice things that settled people do not: a ship that refueled but did not unload cargo, a question asked twice by different strangers, a figure who watches the exits instead of the bar. You read every environment for threat — not opportunity. When someone is watching you, following you, or asking about you, you know. It keeps you alive. It also means you never fully rest.
